description: COMPLETE emissions file for CEN-Chile (Sistema Eléctrico Nacional) project-specific overrides on top of the bundled IPCC-2006 defaults. Carries the full IPCC 2006 defaults (15 fuels from the bundled file) PLUS Chile-specific overrides (sub-bituminous coal, LNG upstream chain, pipeline-NG split).  This is a STANDALONE file — pass it via --emissions-file and it provides EVERY pollutant default the engine needs.  Honour Chilean fuel-sourcing facts that the generic IPCC defaults miss: ALL Chilean coal plants burn sub-bituminous (Andean + Colombian imports), and LNG carries a real ~10 kgCO2/GJ upstream chain (liquefaction + tanker + regas) that's not in the IPCC stationary-combustion default. Pair with `plp2gtopt --plexos-overlay … --emissions --emissions-file share/gtopt/emissions/cen_chile.json` OR `plexos2gtopt --emissions --emissions-file share/gtopt/emissions/cen_chile.json` for CEN bundles.
source: Same IPCC 2006 Vol 2 base values as the bundled defaults file at `scripts/gtopt_shared/data/ipcc_emission_factors.json` — this file ONLY overrides the per-fuel sub-grade assignment for the Chilean fleet. The Gas-LNG-vs-pipeline split is handled automatically by `plexos2gtopt::extract_fuels` via the `_GN_` name-suffix detection (sets `FuelSpec.subtype`); this file backs that up for downstream tools that don't run the parser.

units.co2_upstream: tCO2 / GJ — LNG upstream is non-zero here (typical median 10 kgCO2/GJ = 0.01) reflecting liquefaction + tanker + regas; pipeline NG keeps the IPCC default 0.

notes: ALL Chilean coal plants burn sub-bituminous coal (Andean + Colombian imports — NOT bituminous). The `coal` alias here points at sub-bituminous (0.0961 tCO2/GJ) rather than the IPCC `coal_bituminous` default (0.0946) — a ~1.5% upward correction.

LNG carries a project-specific upstream chain in Chile: GNL Quintero + GNL Mejillones both source from international LNG markets via tanker. The `co2_upstream` value 0.01 (= 10 kgCO2/GJ) is a market-median estimate; refine via per-cargo LCA when carbon accounting matters.
Pipeline natural gas (Gas_*_GN_*) keeps `co2_upstream = 0` per IPCC default — domestic Argentine + Chilean Magallanes pipelines have minimal upstream beyond extraction.
Diesel / FuelOil / Biomass / Biogas / GLP / Petcoke entries here just re-export the bundled defaults unchanged — same fuel families CEN uses, no Chile-specific override needed.

heat_content for `coal_sub_bituminous` here is 25.8 GJ/t (Chilean operational NCV) NOT 18.9 (IPCC sub-bit textbook).  This matches Colombian-import coal procurement specs.  Using 18.9 with PLEXOS heat_rate (already calibrated against actual fuel) under-counts CO2 by ~25%.

fuels.name: diesel
fuels.ipcc_reference: Table 1.4 — Gas/Diesel Oil (74100 kg CO2/TJ); Table 1.2 NCV 43.0 TJ/Gg; Table 2.2 — Gas/Diesel Oil (3 kg CH4/TJ, 0.6 kg N2O/TJ)

fuels.name: fuel_oil
fuels.ipcc_reference: Table 1.4 — Residual Fuel Oil (77400 kg CO2/TJ); Table 1.2 NCV 40.4 TJ/Gg; Table 2.2 — Residual Fuel Oil (3, 0.6)

fuels.name: natural_gas
fuels.ipcc_reference: Table 1.4 — Natural Gas (56100 kg CO2/TJ); Table 2.2 — Natural Gas (1, 0.1). CEN override: distinguishes pipeline gas (this entry, upstream = 0) from LNG (above, upstream = 0.01). Combustion factor identical at the burner tip.

fuels.name: lng
fuels.ipcc_reference: Table 1.4 — Natural Gas at the burner tip (56100 kg CO2/TJ); Table 2.2 — Natural Gas (1, 0.1). CEN override: co2_upstream = 0.01 tCO2/GJ = 10 kgCO2/GJ — market-median LNG-chain factor (liquefaction + tanker + regas), missing from the IPCC stationary-combustion default.

fuels.name: coal_bituminous
fuels.ipcc_reference: Table 1.4 — Other Bituminous Coal (94600 kg CO2/TJ); Table 1.2 NCV 25.8 TJ/Gg; Table 2.2 — Other Bituminous Coal (1, 1.5)

fuels.name: coal_sub_bituminous
fuels.ipcc_reference: Table 1.4 — Sub-Bituminous Coal combustion factor (96100 kg CO2/TJ); Table 2.2 — Sub-Bituminous Coal CH4 + N2O (1, 1.5).  CEN override: heat_content = 25.8 GJ/t (Chilean operational reality — Colombian imports + Andean coal procurement specs).  IPCC textbook for Sub-Bit is 18.9 GJ/t but Chilean coal procurement contracts run 24-26 GJ/t — using 18.9 with PLEXOS-side heat_rate (already calibrated against actual coal NCV) gives ~25% LOW per-MWh CO2.

fuels.name: coal_anthracite
fuels.ipcc_reference: Table 1.4 — Anthracite (98300 kg CO2/TJ); Table 1.2 NCV 26.7 TJ/Gg; Table 2.2 — Anthracite (1, 1.5)

fuels.name: coal_lignite
fuels.ipcc_reference: Table 1.4 — Lignite (101000 kg CO2/TJ); Table 1.2 NCV 11.9 TJ/Gg; Table 2.2 — Lignite (1, 1.5)

fuels.name: coking_coal
fuels.ipcc_reference: Table 1.4 — Coking Coal (94600 kg CO2/TJ); Table 1.2 NCV 28.2 TJ/Gg; Table 2.2 — Coking Coal (1, 1.5)

fuels.name: petcoke
fuels.ipcc_reference: Table 1.4 — Petroleum Coke (97500 kg CO2/TJ); Table 1.2 NCV 32.5 TJ/Gg; Table 2.2 — Petroleum Coke (3, 0.6 — defaults to liquid-fuel row)

fuels.name: lpg
fuels.ipcc_reference: Table 1.4 — Liquefied Petroleum Gases (63100 kg CO2/TJ); Table 1.2 NCV 47.3 TJ/Gg; Table 2.2 — LPG (1, 0.1)

fuels.name: kerosene
fuels.ipcc_reference: Table 1.4 — Other Kerosene (71900 kg CO2/TJ); Table 1.2 NCV 43.8 TJ/Gg; Table 2.2 — Other Kerosene (3, 0.6)

fuels.name: naphtha
fuels.ipcc_reference: Table 1.4 — Naphtha (73300 kg CO2/TJ); Table 1.2 NCV 44.5 TJ/Gg; Table 2.2 — Naphtha (3, 0.6 — defaults to liquid-fuel row)

fuels.name: crude_oil
fuels.ipcc_reference: Table 1.4 — Crude Oil (73300 kg CO2/TJ); Table 1.2 NCV 42.3 TJ/Gg; Table 2.2 — Crude Oil (3, 0.6)

fuels.name: biomass
fuels.ipcc_reference: Table 1.4 — Wood/Wood Waste (112000 kg CO2/TJ gross); biogenic-zero default per AFOLU-sector accounting (combustion CO2 offset by sustainable uptake). Use co2_combustion_gross for a non-zero biomass scenario.; Table 2.2 — Wood/Wood Waste (30 kg CH4/TJ, 4 kg N2O/TJ — NOT biogenic-zero like CO2: combustion byproducts)

fuels.name: biogas
fuels.ipcc_reference: Table 1.4 — Other Biogas (54600 kg CO2/TJ gross). Biogenic-zero default; use co2_combustion_gross when biogenic CO2 must be priced.; Table 2.2 — Other Biogas (1, 0.1)

fuels.name: geothermal
fuels.ipcc_reference: Geothermal is non-combustion. IPCC stationary-combustion default = 0. Non-condensable-gas releases at specific fields are site-specific and not part of an IPCC default.; Geothermal: non-combustion (0, 0)
